npcs.name: Achilles
npcs.role: Prince of Phthia, son of Thetis — the greatest warrior of the age
npcs.description: Son of the sea-nymph [[Thetis]] and mortal king Peleus. He was given a choice at birth: a long, obscure life or a short, glorious one. He chose glory. Now in his rage at [[Agamemnon]] he may choose wrong again — and take the [[The Achaean Host|Achaeans]] down with him. His grief at [[Patroclus]]'s death will shake heaven.

log.note: Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon dishonors Apollo's priest — the god's anger follows.
[[Achilles]] calls an assembly and demands [[Agamemnon]] explain the plague; he is the only one willing to name what everyone knows.
Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon seizes Briseis from Achilles — the rupture that turns the war.
[[Achilles]] nearly kills [[Agamemnon]]; [[Athena]] stops him. He withdraws from battle, prays to [[Thetis]] to beg Zeus to let the Achaeans suffer, and retreats to his ships.

[[Achilles]] refuses everything. He says: two fates await him — return home and live long, or stay and die young with glory. He is choosing neither right now. He will decide when [[Hector]] burns the ships.
Consequence of Patroclus (ally): Patroclus is killed by Hector — Achilles' armor stripped, the bond severed.
[[Achilles]] learns [[Patroclus]] is dead. He collapses. His grief is absolute. [[Thetis]] rises from the sea to hold him. He tells her: I will kill [[Hector]] and then I will die.
[[Achilles]] returns to battle in new divine armor; [[The City of Troy|the Trojans]] rout at the sight of him. He kills everything in his path. The river Scamander runs red.
[[Achilles]] kills [[Hector]]. He drags the body behind his chariot around Troy's walls for twelve days, visiting [[Patroclus]]'s tomb. The gods watch in pain. [[Priam]] watches from the walls.
Consequence of Hector (rival): Hector is killed by Achilles — his body desecrated, Troy's future severed.
[[Achilles]] weeps with [[Priam]] — each man weeping for what he has lost. He lifts the old king from the ground. He promises twelve days of truce. He returns [[Hector]]'s body without ransom.

npcs.name: Agamemnon
npcs.role: High King of Mycenae, commander of the Achaean forces
npcs.description: King of kings by wealth and bloodline, not merit. He started this war to reclaim his brother's wife and has kept one hundred thousand men from home for a decade. He is capable of grandeur and of pettiness in equal measure. Stripping Briseis from [[Achilles]] is the worst decision of the war.

log.note: Chryses, priest of [[Apollo]], comes to ransom his daughter Chryseis; [[Agamemnon]] dismisses him with contempt and threats, dishonoring [[Apollo]]'s servant.
[[Agamemnon]] returns Chryseis but seizes Briseis from [[Achilles]] as compensation; a calculated humiliation of the man he most needs and most resents.
[[Agamemnon]]'s offer is enormous in treasure and zero in apology; [[Achilles]] reads the pride beneath the gifts and says no to all of it.
Consequence of Achilles (ally): Achilles returns to battle — divine armor, Patroclus unavenged, the Trojans route.

npcs.name: Patroclus
npcs.role: Companion of Achilles, the gentlest of the Greeks
npcs.description: [[Achilles]]' closest friend — some say his soul's other half. He killed a boy in a quarrel as a child and was sent to [[Achilles]]' father's court in exile, where the two grew up together. He is braver than he should be and more compassionate than the war deserves. He will die wearing [[Achilles]]' armor.

log.note: [[Patroclus]] comes to [[Achilles]] with tears and begs to fight in his armor; [[Achilles]] relents, giving the Myrmidons and his divine horses, but warns him: drive [[The City of Troy|the Trojans]] back and then return.
[[Patroclus]] fights brilliantly in [[Achilles]]' armor, driving [[The City of Troy|Trojans]] back from the ships; then, ignoring his friend's command, he presses on toward Troy's walls.
[[Patroclus]] dies. His last words to [[Hector]]: death is coming for you too, and it is not far off. [[Hector]] strips [[Achilles]]' divine armor from the body.

npcs.name: Odysseus
npcs.description: The wiliest man in the Greek world. He argued against joining the expedition and was tricked into going. Now he serves as strategist, diplomat, and the voice of cold reason in a war run on hot pride. He survives everything by thinking three moves ahead.

log.note: Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon dishonors Apollo's priest — the god's anger follows.
Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon seizes Briseis from Achilles — the rupture that turns the war.

[[Odysseus]], [[Ajax]], and Phoenix carry [[Agamemnon]]'s offer to [[Achilles]]: treasure, Briseis returned, [[Agamemnon]]'s daughter in marriage. [[Odysseus]] presents it brilliantly. [[Achilles]] sees the pride beneath the gifts.

npcs.name: Ajax
npcs.role: Prince of Salamis, bulwark of the Greeks
npcs.description: The largest and second-strongest of the Greeks. Where [[Achilles]] is a force of nature, Ajax is a wall — steadfast, immovable, the last man standing when others break. He will hold the ships against [[Hector]] alone. He carries a shield like a tower and feels the weight of every man he cannot save.

log.note: [[Ajax]] stands alone at the ships and drives off [[Hector]]'s assault; he is the last thing between the fleet and fire.
Consequence of Achilles (ally): Achilles returns to battle — divine armor, Patroclus unavenged, the Trojans route.

npcs.name: Menelaus
npcs.description: This war was nominally fought to bring back his wife. He is brave but outclassed by the war his brother [[Agamemnon]]'s armies waged in his name. When he finally duels [[Paris]] — the man who stole [[Helen]] — he nearly wins. Aphrodite saves [[Paris]] and Menelaus is left holding nothing but his anger.

log.note: Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon dishonors Apollo's priest — the god's anger follows.
Consequence of Agamemnon (ally): Agamemnon seizes Briseis from Achilles — the rupture that turns the war.
[[Menelaus]] fights [[Paris]] in single combat; he seizes [[Paris]] by the helmet and is about to drag him to death when Aphrodite snaps the chin-strap and spirits [[Paris]] away.

npcs.name: Diomedes
npcs.role: King of Argos, the most fearless fighter
npcs.description: The most effective Achaean fighter during [[Achilles]]' absence. He wounds Aphrodite. He wounds Ares. He does things no mortal should do and survives them. [[Athena]] walks beside him, unseen, during his aristeia. He is what heroism looks like without the tragic weight.

log.note: With [[Athena]] beside him [[Diomedes]] tears through the Trojan line, wounding Aphrodite's wrist and driving his spear into Ares' flank. He is doing the impossible.

npcs.name: Nestor
npcs.description: The oldest of the Greek kings, full of stories about wars he fought in his youth that dwarf this one. His advice is almost always right; it is almost never followed fast enough. He is the institutional memory of what honor requires and what it costs.

npcs.name: Hector
npcs.role: Prince of Troy, champion of the Trojans
npcs.description: [[Priam]]'s eldest son and Troy's defender. He fights not for glory but for his city, his wife, his infant son. He knows Troy will fall. He goes out each morning anyway. The Iliad's moral center — the poem loves him even as it kills him. His farewell to [[Andromache]] at [[The Scaean Gate]] is everything.

log.note: [[Hector]] enters Troy to arrange sacrifice; he finds [[Paris]] idle at home with [[Helen]] and shames him back to battle. He goes to find [[Andromache]].

At [[The Scaean Gate]] [[Hector]] bids [[Andromache]] farewell; she begs him to stay. He lifts their son Astyanax, who recoils from the great shining helmet. He prays the boy will be greater than his father. He goes back to war.
[[Hector]] kills [[Patroclus]] in single combat and strips [[Achilles]]' divine armor; he puts it on himself, not knowing what the act will cost him.
Consequence of Achilles (rival): Achilles returns to battle — divine armor, Patroclus unavenged, the Trojans route.
[[Hector]] stands outside the gates as all of Troy flees inside. His father [[Priam]] begs from the walls; his mother tears her robes. He waits for [[Achilles]].

[[Hector]] runs three circuits of Troy pursued by [[Achilles]]; [[Athena]] takes Deiphobus's form to stop him. He turns and faces his death with full knowledge. He asks only to be given back for burial. [[Achilles]] refuses.

npcs.name: Andromache
npcs.role: Wife of Hector, voice of grief
npcs.description: Her father and seven brothers were killed by [[Achilles]] before the poem begins. She has no one left but [[Hector]]. She begs him not to fight; she knows exactly how this ends. Her lament over his body is among the most devastating passages in Western literature.

log.note: [[Andromache]] tells [[Hector]] he is her father, her mother, her brothers — everyone she has left. She watches him walk away knowing it is the last time.
Consequence of Hector (ally): Hector is killed by Achilles — his body desecrated, Troy's future severed.

[[Andromache]] faints upon the wall when she sees [[Hector]]'s body dragged behind [[Achilles]]' chariot. She weeps not for Troy but for their son Astyanax, who will grow up fatherless in a fallen city.

npcs.name: Priam
npcs.role: King of Troy, father of fifty sons
npcs.description: He has watched his city become a prison and his sons die one by one. His dignity is extraordinary — he walks alone into [[Achilles]]' camp to beg for [[Hector]]'s body, and [[Achilles]] weeps with him. The poem's final act is between these two men who have every reason to be enemies.

log.note: Consequence of Hector (ally): Hector is killed by Achilles — his body desecrated, Troy's future severed.
[[Priam]] watches his greatest son [[Hector]] die from the walls. He tears his white hair and weeps in the dust. Troy's fathers watch with him.

[[Priam]] loads a wagon with treasure, guided by Hermes, and walks alone into the enemy camp at night. He kneels before [[Achilles]] and asks him to remember his own father. He kisses the hands that killed his son.

npcs.name: Paris
npcs.role: Prince of Troy, the cause of everything
npcs.description: [[Hector]]'s younger brother. He was given a choice by the gods — wisdom, power, or beauty — and chose beauty: Aphrodite and [[Helen]]. Now a hundred thousand men are dead for his decision and he fights the consequences with a bow, at a distance, never quite accepting blame. He kills [[Achilles]] in the end. He never deserves to.

log.note: [[Paris]] challenges the assembled Greeks to single combat; he nearly flees when [[Menelaus]] actually steps forward — [[Hector]] shames him back.
Aphrodite deposits [[Paris]] in [[Helen]]'s bedroom, unharmed and unrepentant; he does not understand why the world is angry with him.
Consequence of Hector (ally): Hector is killed by Achilles — his body desecrated, Troy's future severed.

npcs.name: Helen
npcs.role: Most beautiful woman in the world, unwilling prize
npcs.description: She watches the war from [[The Walls of Troy|the walls of Troy]], naming Greek heroes for [[Priam]]. Did she choose to go with [[Paris]] or was she taken? Homer leaves it deliberately unclear. She hates herself, or says she does. [[Priam]] is kind to her. She is not the cause of the war — she is its most visible wound.

log.note: [[Helen]] is furious at Aphrodite for saving [[Paris]]; she knows she should go back to [[Menelaus]] and cannot; the goddess will not let her. She is trapped by beauty and by god.

npcs.name: Apollo
npcs.role: God of sun and plague, champion of Troy
npcs.description: The archer god. He sent the plague that opened the poem and has sheltered Troy throughout. He fights on the Trojan side openly, deflects spears from [[Hector]], and finally guides [[Paris]]'s arrow into [[Achilles]]' heel. The gods play by rules that are not human rules.

log.note: Consequence of Agamemnon (rival): Agamemnon dishonors Apollo's priest — the god's anger follows.
[[Apollo]] descends from Olympus and looses plague arrows into the Achaean camp; for nine days the pyres burn without ceasing as men and mules die.
Consequence of Agamemnon (rival): Agamemnon seizes Briseis from Achilles — the rupture that turns the war.
[[Apollo]] strikes [[Patroclus]] from behind on the battlefield, knocking away his helmet and snapping his spear; Euphorbus wounds him from behind; [[Hector]] drives in the killing blow.
Consequence of Hector (ally): Hector is killed by Achilles — his body desecrated, Troy's future severed.

npcs.name: Athena
npcs.role: Goddess of wisdom and war, patron of the Greeks
npcs.description: She appears to [[Achilles]] in Book 1 to stop him from killing [[Agamemnon]]. She walks beside [[Diomedes]] during his aristeia. She tricks [[Hector]] into standing to face [[Achilles]] by disguising herself as his brother. She is the most active divine presence in the poem.

log.note: Consequence of Achilles (ally): Achilles returns to battle — divine armor, Patroclus unavenged, the Trojans route.

npcs.name: Thetis
npcs.description: [[Achilles]]' divine mother. She knows he is fated to die young and has spent his life trying to delay it. She persuades Zeus to favor the Trojans while [[Achilles]] withdraws, which leads to [[Patroclus]]'s death. Her grief and [[Achilles]]' grief mirror each other across the poem.

log.note: [[Thetis]] rises from the sea and holds her weeping son; she promises to go to Zeus. She knows he will die young. She grants his wish anyway.
[[Thetis]] goes to Hephaestus and commissions new armor for her son; she knows she is equipping him for his death.
Consequence of Achilles (ally): Achilles returns to battle — divine armor, Patroclus unavenged, the Trojans route.
